tasks.title: Instrument rendered list item editing with debug logging
tasks.description: Add targeted debug logging around rendered-mode list item click handling, index mapping, and edit-state key generation to isolate the off-by-one/indexing bug.
tasks.details:
Implementation details:
- Work primarily in `src/markdown/editor.rs`, with supporting logs in `src/markdown/parser.rs`, `src/markdown/widgets.rs`, and `src/state.rs` where list-related indexing or edit state is touched.
- Identify the full click-to-edit pipeline for rendered list items:
  - UI event → click position → mapped markdown AST node (list item).
  - Generation of any structural keys/IDs used for the `HashMap<String, FormattedItemEditState>`.
  - Retrieval and mutation of `FormattedItemEditState` when user types.
- In `render_list_item()` and `render_list_item_with_structural_keys()`:
  - Log the logical list index (e.g., `list_idx`, `item_idx`) and any enumerate counter.
  - Log the structural key/ID string used as the HashMap key.
  - Log a compact description of the underlying AST node (e.g., list depth, ordered/unordered, text preview).
  - Ensure logging is behind a feature flag or debug level (e.g., `tracing` or `log` crate; use `debug!`), so release builds are not noisy.
- At the click-handler boundary for rendered mode:
  - Log incoming click coordinates (or widget index) and the resolved target node index/ID.
  - Log when an item enters editing state: which structural key and which AST index it maps to.
- In the code that reads/writes `FormattedItemEditState` in `src/state.rs`:
  - Log when an edit state is created, updated, or removed, including the key and any associated AST reference.
- Add a debug-only helper function to dump list structures for a document:
  - Traverse the markdown AST list nodes, printing a deterministic index (including nesting) and the generated structural keys.
  - Use the minimal reproduction markdown from the PRD to visually verify index vs key mapping.
- Ensure logs make it easy to compare rendered-mode behavior to raw mode:
  - Instrument raw-mode indexing paths with similar logs (without changing behavior).
- Guard all new logging with `cfg!(debug_assertions)` or a `debug_rendered_click_to_edit` feature flag to avoid performance regression in production builds.

Pseudo-code sketch:
```rust
// in render_list_item_with_structural_keys
fn render_list_item_with_structural_keys(ctx: &mut Ctx, item: &AstNode, list_idx: usize, item_idx: usize, key_prefix: &str) {
    let key = format!("{key_prefix}:{list_idx}:{item_idx}");
    log::debug!(
        "render_list_item: list_idx={}, item_idx={}, key={}, text_preview={}",
        list_idx,
        item_idx,
        key,
        text_preview(item),
    );
    // existing rendering code...
}

// click handler
fn on_rendered_click(pos: ClickPosition) {
    let hit = map_click_to_ast_node(pos);
    log::debug!(
        "rendered_click: pos={:?}, ast_index={:?}, key={:?}",
        pos,
        hit.ast_index,
        hit.structural_key,
    );
    // existing edit-start logic...
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
- Build a debug build with logging enabled.
- Use the provided minimal markdown test file plus an additional nested-list variant.
- Manually click:
  - First list item under a header.
  - Second, third, and last items in the list.
  - Items in nested lists.
- Verify logs show:
  - Clicked visual item index matches AST node index and structural key for that exact item (no off-by-one between header and first item).
  - No case where editing for index N logs an edit state keyed to index N-1 or another item.
- Compare logs from rendered mode vs raw mode for the same document to see where indices diverge.
- Once the bug is understood, capture before/after logs as evidence for the fix.

tasks.title: Correct list item index mapping between click position and markdown AST
tasks.description: Fix the logic that maps rendered click positions to markdown AST list item nodes so that the correct list item (including the first) is selected.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Use the minimal markdown test file specified in the PRD.
- Verify manually in rendered mode:
  - Clicking the first list item always selects that item, never the header above.
  - Clicking each subsequent list item selects that exact item; typed text appears only in the clicked item.
- Add a unit test (or integration test if available) that:
  - Constructs a markdown AST with header + list + another header + list.
  - Simulates click indices for each list item and asserts that `map_click_to_ast_node` returns the correct AST node ID for each.
- Add nested list cases in tests to confirm that depth and child indices are correctly resolved for each item.
- Re-run the debug logs introduced in task 64 to ensure no remaining off-by-one discrepancies between hit indices and AST indices.

tasks.title: Fix structural key and edit-state HashMap key generation for list items
tasks.description: Align structural key/ID generation for list items with the corrected AST index mapping so each visible list item has a unique, stable key that points to the right edit state.
tasks.testStrategy:
- With debug logging still enabled, confirm that for each clicked list item:
  - The structural key logged at click time matches the key used during render when the item is in edit mode.
  - Different items (including nested ones) never share the same structural key.
- Manual tests in rendered mode:
  - Click first, second, and third items; type distinct text in each; ensure text stays scoped to the correct items when switching focus between them.
  - Add and edit items in nested lists; verify each nested item maintains its own edit state and key.
- Add unit tests for key generation:
  - Given several synthetic `ListPath` values, assert that `formatted_item_key` produces distinct, deterministic keys.
  - If using `NodeId`, assert that items with different `NodeId` values always map to different keys.
- Regression check:
  - Verify that other formatted content (e.g., bold, italic, code segments) that uses `FormattedItemEditState` still resolves keys correctly and remains editable.

tasks.title: Ensure consistent rendered-mode list editing behavior including nested lists
tasks.description: Normalize rendered-mode list editing behavior across all list items (1st through Nth) and nesting levels, ensuring edits always affect the clicked item only, based on the verified non-structural-key rendering path currently in use.
tasks.details:
Implementation details:
- Treat this task as primarily **verification and documentation of the current, working implementation** using the non-structural-keys path (`render_node` → `render_list` → `render_list_item`).

- End-to-end rendered-mode list editing flow (current, verified behavior):
  - Click detection uses `sense_response.clicked()` inside `render_list_item` to start editing when a list item is clicked.
  - AST node resolution is based on `para.start_line` (with the frontmatter offset fix from Task 66) to map the click to the correct list item node.
  - A unique per-item key is generated via `ui.id().with("formatted_list_item").with(para.start_line).with(item_number)`, ensuring globally unique IDs for all list items, including nested ones.
  - `FormattedItemEditState` is stored in egui's persistent memory and holds the edit buffer so text is not lost across frame rebuilds or re-renders.
  - The TextEdit widget is wired to the `FormattedItemEditState` buffer and receives focus via a `needs_focus` flag and `request_focus()`.

- Single active item editing behavior (current, verified behavior):
  - Rely on egui's native focus management so that only one TextEdit can be focused at a time.
  - On focus loss, a `focus_lost` condition triggers commit and exit from edit mode for the previously focused item.
  - When committing, the edit state flag (`editing = false`) is cleared and the updated text is written back to the corresponding markdown AST node.
  - Clicking a second item causes the first item's TextEdit to lose focus → the first item commits and exits edit mode → the second item enters edit mode.

- Nested list handling (current, verified behavior):
  - Nested lists are rendered via recursive `render_list` calls with `indent_level + 1`.
  - Because `para.start_line` values are unique per source line and combined with `item_number`, each list item (including nested ones) receives a globally unique ID and maintains its own `FormattedItemEditState`.
  - Nested items are independently editable with no leakage of edits between parents, siblings, or different nesting levels.
  - Visual depth is controlled by `indent_width = indent_level * 20.0`, ensuring depth-aware indentation without affecting the edit-state routing.

- Headers and other non-list elements (current, verified behavior):
  - Headers use a different ID prefix (e.g., `"formatted_paragraph"`), while list items use `"formatted_list_item"`, so their IDs and edit states are isolated.
  - Clicks on headings follow their own click-to-edit path and do not share list-state structures or keys.
  - List editing logic does not cause headings to enter edit mode when interacting with list items.

- Formatted spans compatibility (current, verified behavior):
  - A `has_inline_formatting` check determines whether a list item contains inline formatting (bold, italic, code, etc.).
  - In display mode, inline elements are rendered via `render_inline_node`.
  - In edit mode, the item shows raw markdown text using `extract_list_item_content`, while still preserving the underlying inline formatting semantics in the AST.
  - The internal representation of formatted spans is unchanged; only parent list item selection and edit-state routing are handled by this task.

- Structural-key rendering path (current status and scope for this task):
  - A structural-keys-based rendering variant (e.g., `render_list_item_with_structural_keys`) exists but is **currently disabled** (referenced only in comments near the top-level render path).
  - The active implementation that users see is the non-structural-keys path described above; this task does **not** require enabling or updating the structural-keys path.
  - The structural-keys version does **not** yet incorporate the newer edit buffer persistence behavior; this is explicitly out of scope for this task and tracked separately.

- State and commit behavior:
  - Edit buffer persistence is handled entirely via egui memory so that intermediate edits are not lost when the UI is rebuilt.
  - Commits are deferred until focus is lost or an explicit commit/exit condition occurs, preventing unintended commits during active typing.
  - When a commit happens, the updated buffer is written back to the correct AST node derived from `para.start_line` and `item_number`, and the associated `FormattedItemEditState` is marked as not editing.

- Known deferred issues (documented but **not** to be fixed under this task):
  - The first formatted list item sometimes has its cursor jump to the end when entering edit mode (minor UX issue tracked in a separate task).
  - The structural-keys version of list-item rendering has not been updated to use the new edit buffer persistence model and remains disabled.

- Implementation expectations for closing this task:
  - Keep the current non-structural-key implementation as the primary, verified path.
  - Make only minimal, targeted adjustments if additional edge cases are found during QA (e.g., unusual nested list combinations), without changing key/ID schemes or the underlying span representation.
  - Ensure all behavior described above remains true after any such adjustments and is reflected in inline comments or brief code documentation where appropriate.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Manual QA using the test markdown file from the PRD and additional nested-list files:
  - Click each item in a flat list (including first and last) and edit it; verify that only the clicked item enters edit mode and that its text stays in the clicked item only after commit.
  - Click and edit items in nested lists (e.g., sublists under a parent item); verify that each nested item is independently editable and that edits never appear in parent or sibling items.
  - Alternate between editing different items (including jumping between different nesting levels); ensure that when focus moves from item A to item B, A commits and exits edit mode and B alone is in edit mode.
  - Confirm that the visual indentation (`indent_level` / `indent_width`) does not affect which item’s text is edited or committed.

- Header and non-list regression checks:
  - Click various headings and verify that they enter their own edit mode using the `"formatted_paragraph"` path, and that list editing behavior does not activate when interacting with headings.
  - Verify that clicking list items never causes a heading to become editable or share edit state.

- Formatted spans checks:
  - For list items containing bold, italic, or inline code, confirm that display mode renders via `render_inline_node` and that edit mode shows raw markdown text via `extract_list_item_content`.
  - Edit text in such items and commit; verify that the resulting markdown preserves the expected inline formatting and that no span structure is lost or corrupted.

- Focus and single-edit-mode behavior:
  - Start editing a list item, then click elsewhere in the document (another list item, a header, or empty space) and verify that egui focus loss triggers commit and exit from edit mode for the original item.
  - Confirm visually that only one TextEdit is focused at any time and that the `editing` flag in the associated state is cleared on commit.

- Logging / instrumentation (if log hooks are available):
  - Optionally enable debug logs for edit lifecycle events and confirm that:
    - Each start/edit/commit event logs the correct list item identification (based on `para.start_line` and `item_number`).
    - No edit event for one item logs an update to another item's key or AST node.

- Non-goals / regression guardrails:
  - Do not enable the structural-keys rendering path as part of this task; smoke-test the build to ensure the disabled code path remains non-breaking.
  - Confirm via `cargo check` and `cargo build --release` that the code compiles successfully after any changes and that the verified behaviors above still hold.

tasks.title: Verify and preserve raw mode behavior, formatting edits, and undo/redo with new list editing logic
tasks.description: Confirm that raw markdown editing, existing formatted content editing, and undo/redo remain correct and stable after the list index and key fixes, and that the app-level undo/redo wiring for rendered mode and the TreeViewer correctly records edits to the Tab history without regressing list-related behavior.
tasks.testStrategy:
Updated test strategy:

- Raw mode tests (regression verification):
  - Open the same markdown file in raw mode using `EditorWidget`.
  - Edit list items and non-list content; verify behavior is unchanged from pre-fix behavior and that Ctrl+Z/Ctrl+Y work as expected in raw mode.
  - Confirm, via logs or debug assertions if available, that `tab.record_edit()` is invoked from raw mode and that undo/redo restores the previous raw text exactly.

- Formatted content tests within list items:
  - In rendered mode, create list items with combinations of bold, italic, and inline code using `FormattedItemEditState`.
  - Edit these list items and verify:
    - Formatted segments remain visually correct after edits.
    - Underlying markdown remains correct for the inline spans.
    - Undo/redo in rendered mode correctly restores both the visual formatting and the raw markdown for these list items.

- Undo/redo tests for rendered mode and TreeViewer (focus of fix):
  - In rendered mode (MarkdownEditor):
    - Perform multiple edits across distinct list items and non-list paragraphs.
    - Use Ctrl+Z repeatedly and verify each step reverts exactly the last change, with no content shifting between list items or sections.
    - Use redo to reapply changes and confirm the same sequence is restored.
    - Specifically verify that edits made only in rendered mode are now undoable (regression against the previous bug).
  - In TreeViewer:
    - Make structural or textual edits via the TreeViewer that affect `tab.content`.
    - Confirm that each change is now placed on the undo stack and that undo/redo restores the previous serialized content.

- Automated tests:
  - Ensure existing tests continue to pass:
    - `cargo test state::tests::test_tab_undo_redo`.
    - `cargo test state::tests::test_tab_record_edit`.
  - If feasible, add or extend tests around the rendered-mode and TreeViewer integration to:
    - Simulate edits through the same paths used by `MarkdownEditor` and `TreeViewer`.
    - Assert that `tab.content` snapshots are recorded when `changed` is true, and that undo/redo restores prior snapshots.

- Build and integration checks:
  - Run `cargo check` and `cargo build --release` to confirm no compilation regressions from the app-level changes in `src/app.rs`.
  - Confirm that the current test suite status (e.g., 548/549 passing with one known, unrelated failure in outline italic heading extraction) remains stable and that no new failures are introduced by the undo/redo wiring.

- Logging/diagnostic verification:
  - Use debug logs in `src/app.rs` for MarkdownEditor and TreeViewer to confirm that `"Content modified in rendered editor, recorded for undo"` (or equivalent) messages appear when edits are made.
  - Cross-check log sequences with manual undo/redo operations to ensure that each user-visible change corresponds to a recorded snapshot in `Tab` history.

tasks.title: Add regression tests and documentation for rendered-mode list editing
tasks.description: Create automated regression tests and developer documentation to prevent future list editing index bugs in rendered mode.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Run the new automated tests in CI and ensure they pass consistently on all supported platforms.
- Manually break index mapping or key generation (e.g., reintroduce an off-by-one) to confirm that the new tests fail, validating their effectiveness as regressions.
- Ask another developer to follow the new documentation to:
  - Understand how rendered list editing works.
  - Add a small new list-editing-related feature or test, confirming that the documentation is clear and actionable.
- Ensure CI is configured to run these tests on every change affecting `src/markdown/editor.rs`, `src/markdown/parser.rs`, `src/markdown/widgets.rs`, or `src/state.rs`.

tasks.title: Fix light mode contrast and add separator between editor and tabs
tasks.description: Audit and correct light mode color usage to meet WCAG contrast guidelines and add a visible separator between the editor area and the tabs region.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:
- Establish target guidelines
  - Adopt WCAG 2.1 level AA for contrast: at least 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.[3][5]
  - For UI components (borders, icons, separators, focus outlines, non-text indicators), ensure at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors.[1][4][8]
  - Decide whether any key surfaces should aim for enhanced (AAA) contrast (7:1 normal text, 4.5:1 large text) for extra legibility.[3][5][6]

- Inventory and audit light mode UI
  - Identify a single source of truth for light mode colors (design tokens/theme file/variables) for:
    - Backgrounds (app background, editor background, sidebars, toolbars, tabs strip, panels).
    - Text (body text, headings, code text, muted/secondary text, placeholders, labels, disabled text).
    - UI elements (borders, dividers/separators, focus rings, hover/active states, badges, buttons, tab backgrounds and indicators, scrollbars if themed).
  - Use a contrast checker (e.g., WebAIM, MDN-recommended tools) to measure contrast between each foreground and its actual runtime background.[4][5][6]
  - Document all failing combinations (text-on-background <4.5:1, large text <3:1, UI/borders/indicators <3:1) with before/after color pairs to be changed.

- Redesign color tokens for light mode
  - Adjust or introduce design tokens for light mode such as `color.textPrimaryLight`, `color.textSecondaryLight`, `color.borderSubtleLight`, `color.borderStrongLight`, `color.bgEditorLight`, `color.bgTabsLight`, etc.
  - For each token, confirm its intended use and ensure that all common pairings (e.g., textPrimary on bgEditorLight, textSecondary on bgPanelLight) satisfy WCAG AA contrast.
  - Ensure that:
    - Primary body text on all standard light backgrounds meets ≥4.5:1.
    - Headings and large text on light backgrounds meet ≥3:1.
    - Placeholder and secondary text are still legible (≥4.5:1 where they convey important information; only relax contrast where text is clearly decorative or non-essential per WCAG exceptions).[3][5]
    - Control outlines, borders, and separator lines (including around inputs, panels, and tabs) meet ≥3:1 relative to adjacent areas.[1][4][8]
  - Avoid using color alone as the only indicator of state; where applicable, reinforce states with weight, underline, shape, or icon changes.

- Implement separator between editor and tabs
  - Decide on visual form:
    - A 1px hairline border or divider between the bottom of the tab bar and top of the editor.
    - Use a `borderStrongLight` token that meets ≥3:1 contrast against both the tabs background and editor background.[1][4][8]
  - Update the layout container around the tabs strip and editor:
    - Add a bottom border to the tabs container, or a top border to the editor container, ensuring it visually reads as a separator.
    - Ensure the divider does not clash with any existing borders (avoid double lines); collapse or refactor existing borders if necessary.
  - Verify that the separator is visible at typical display scales and on low-contrast/older monitors.

- Apply corrected tokens across components
  - Update component styles (CSS, theme objects, or style modules) to consume the revised light mode tokens rather than hard-coded colors.
  - Prioritize elements with known visibility issues in light mode:
    - Tab text and icons, active/inactive tab contrast, and hover/selected states.
    - Editor gutter/line numbers, cursor and selection highlights, code syntax colors if they share the same tokens.
    - Panel headers, footers, and status bars.
    - Buttons (primary, secondary, ghost), links, and focus states.
    - Input fields, dropdowns, checkboxes, toggles, and error/warning states.
  - Confirm that dark mode and high-contrast themes are not unintentionally altered; if separate tokens per theme are used, change only the light mode set.

- Document and align with design/dev teams
  - Add a short "Light Mode Contrast" section in the design or developer docs:
    - State the chosen WCAG level, minimum ratios, and which tokens are intended for text vs borders vs backgrounds.[3][5]
    - Provide examples of correct pairings and a short do/don't list.
  - Optionally, add a simple script or checklist to run contrast checks against the token list to prevent regressions.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Automated and tooling-based checks
  - Use a WCAG contrast checker (e.g., WebAIM, MDN-listed tools) to test all primary token combinations:
    - Verify primary text on each standard light background has ≥4.5:1 contrast.[3][4][5]
    - Verify large/heading text meets ≥3:1 contrast where applicable.[3][5]
    - Verify borders, dividers (including the new editor/tabs separator), focus rings, and other UI components have ≥3:1 contrast relative to adjacent areas.[1][4][8]
  - If feasible, add a small script or unit test that enumerates token pairs (foreground/background) and asserts that their precomputed ratios meet the chosen thresholds.

- Manual visual QA in light mode
  - Open the app in light mode on at least two displays (e.g., laptop and external monitor) and visually inspect:
    - Global backgrounds and primary content areas for adequate text readability.
    - Tabs area: confirm tab labels, icons, active/hover states are clearly distinguishable.
    - Editor area: ensure body text, line numbers, selection colors, and any inline status or hints are readable.
  - Specifically verify the separator between editor and tabs:
    - Confirm there is a single, clearly visible divider line (or equivalent) between the tab strip and editor.
    - Check that the separator remains visible when a document is empty, when scrolled, and when switching between tabs.
  - Review forms and interactive controls:
    - Inputs, dropdowns, checkboxes, toggles, and buttons have visible borders/outlines and pass the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement.[1][4][8]
    - Focus states are clearly visible in light mode.

- Accessibility-focused sanity checks
  - Use browser dev tools or an accessibility extension to spot-check contrast warnings on key screens.
  - Have at least one team member with no prior knowledge of the changes perform an informal accessibility pass in light mode and report any elements that still feel low-contrast or hard to distinguish.

- Regression checks
  - Switch between light and dark mode and verify that:
    - Dark mode visuals remain unchanged or within acceptable minor differences.
    - No layout shifts or visual glitches appear around the tabs/editor boundary after introducing the separator.
  - Run the existing automated test suite (including UI or snapshot tests, if present) and update snapshots where they only reflect intentional color/separator changes.

tasks.title: Fix bidirectional scroll synchronization between Raw and Rendered editor modes
tasks.description: Diagnose and repair the broken scroll synchronization between Raw and Rendered markdown views so that scrolling in either view maintains a stable, correlated position in the other.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:
- Assess current scroll sync implementation
  - Locate the existing scroll synchronization logic between Raw and Rendered views (likely in the main editor component/module handling both views and any shared state for scroll positions).
  - Identify event sources (e.g., scroll listeners on the raw textarea/code editor and rendered preview container) and understand how they currently propagate scroll position to the counterpart view.
  - Document current behavior and limitations: one-way only, jitter, feedback loops, incorrect mapping, or no-op in one direction.

- Define scrolling correlation model
  - Decide on the source-of-truth for scroll correlation: e.g., use a shared "virtual document position" or percentage-of-document approach based on total scrollable height of each pane.
  - For a first pass, implement a robust percentage-based mapping:
    - Compute scroll ratio as `scrollTop / (scrollHeight - clientHeight)` in the scrolled pane.
    - Apply this ratio to the target pane as `targetScrollTop = ratio * (targetScrollHeight - targetClientHeight)`.
  - If markdown structure is readily available from existing AST utilities (used in tasks 64–69), consider a follow-up enhancement to map scroll position via block/line anchors for more accurate correlation, but do not block the bug fix on this.

- Implement bidirectional scroll handling
  - Add scroll listeners to **both** Raw and Rendered panes, ensuring they both:
    - Compute the scroll ratio when the user scrolls that pane.
    - Request an update of the other pane's scrollTop based on that ratio.
  - Prevent feedback loops:
    - Introduce a short-lived guard flag or re-entrancy lock (e.g., an enum or boolean) to distinguish user-initiated scrolling from programmatic scrolling.
    - When handling a scroll event in pane A, set `isSyncingFrom = A`, update pane B, and ignore scroll events from B when `isSyncingFrom == A`.
    - Reset the guard after the sync completes, taking care to handle rapid successive events.
  - Ensure that scroll handler registration and cleanup are tied to the editor lifecycle and view-switching logic to avoid dangling listeners.

- Integrate with existing editor state and layout
  - Confirm that scroll sync works correctly when switching between Raw and Rendered modes, including when only one pane is visible versus split view (if applicable).
  - Ensure that any changes from list-editing tasks (64–69) that touch layout or block heights are respected; scroll sync should not assume fixed heights per block but rely on runtime scroll metrics.
  - Avoid introducing additional global state beyond what is necessary for guards and shared scroll ratio; prefer local state in the editor component.

- Performance and UX considerations
  - Use throttling or passive listeners if needed to maintain smooth scrolling, but ensure sync remains visually tight.
  - Avoid layout thrashing by minimizing style reads and writes within each scroll event; batch reads of `scrollHeight`, `clientHeight`, and `scrollTop` before updating the target pane.
  - Verify behavior on large documents and with complex rendered markdown (lists, nested lists, headings, code blocks) so scroll sync remains stable.

- Code organization and documentation
  - Implement sync logic in a dedicated helper or module (e.g., `scroll_sync.rs` or equivalent) to keep editor components clean and facilitate future reuse.
  - Add inline comments explaining the guard/lock mechanism, the correlation model, and any assumptions or constraints.
  - Update developer documentation for the editor to describe how scroll synchronization works, where it is implemented, and how to extend or modify it safely.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Unit and integration-level checks
  - If there is an existing test harness for the editor UI, add tests (or low-level integration tests) that simulate scroll events in each pane and assert that the counterpart pane’s scrollTop is updated according to the chosen mapping.
  - Test edge cases: zero-height content, very short documents (no scroll), and very long documents with large `scrollHeight`.

- Manual QA scenarios
  - Baseline behavior
    - Open a medium-sized markdown document with headings, paragraphs, code blocks, and lists (including nested lists similar to those used in tasks 64–69).
    - Scroll **Raw → Rendered**:
      - Slowly drag the scrollbar from top to bottom in Raw mode and observe the Rendered view.
      - Verify that the Rendered pane tracks the Raw pane’s position smoothly and ends near the same logical point in the document.
    - Scroll **Rendered → Raw**:
      - Repeat the same procedure starting from the Rendered view and confirm the Raw pane stays in sync.
  - Mouse wheel / trackpad tests
    - Use mouse wheel or trackpad scrolling in each pane independently.
    - Confirm the other pane updates continuously, without lag or jumps, and without causing oscillation or "rubber-banding" effects.
  - Rapid and alternating scrolls
    - Rapidly scroll Raw, then immediately scroll Rendered, repeatedly.
    - Verify there are no infinite loops, no jittery back-and-forth updates, and the guard/lock logic prevents feedback cycles.
  - Mode switching and layout changes
    - With both panes visible (if supported), verify sync works in both directions.
    - Switch between split view and single-pane view (Raw only, Rendered only) and back; confirm scroll sync resumes correctly when both panes are visible again.
    - Resize the window while scrolled to various positions and confirm scrolling still syncs correctly after the resize.
  - Structural content tests
    - Use documents with many lists and nested lists (leveraging fixtures from tasks 64–69) and verify that when you position the caret in a particular visible area and scroll one pane, the corresponding content in the other pane is roughly aligned.

- Regression checks
  - Confirm that no new scroll-related bugs are introduced in list editing or other editor features that rely on layout.
  - Run the existing test suite, especially any tests added in tasks 68–69 related to raw-mode behavior and rendered-mode list editing, to ensure scroll sync changes do not interfere with editing logic.

tasks.title: Implement multi-cursor editing (VS Code-style) in editor
tasks.description: Add VS Code-style multiple cursor support to the editor, including Ctrl+D next-occurrence selection, Ctrl+Click cursor addition, and Alt+Click+Drag column selection, with all cursors editing simultaneously.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:
- Design cursor abstraction
  - Extend the existing editor model to represent **multiple carets/selections** instead of a single primary cursor (e.g., `Vec<Selection>` where each selection has anchor/head, direction, and per-cursor metadata like preferred column).
  - Ensure the rendering layer can draw multiple carets and selection highlights, respecting theme colors and existing focus/selection styles.
  - Decide on a canonical "primary" cursor for operations that require a single reference point (e.g., scroll anchoring, status bar position display), while still applying edits to all cursors.

- Keyboard/mouse gesture handling
  - Implement **Ctrl+D** (or platform-appropriate equivalent) to:
    - If no selection: select the word/token under the primary cursor and create a first selection.
    - On repeated presses: find the next non-overlapping occurrence of the current selection text and add a new cursor/selection there.
    - Skip already selected occurrences and stop when no further match exists.
    - Avoid crossing structural boundaries that would break existing AST-based editing assumptions (e.g., don’t cross into other blocks if the editor assumes block-local editing).
  - Implement **Ctrl+Click** to add a new cursor at the clicked text position without removing existing cursors; a plain click should still clear to a single cursor, preserving current behavior.
  - Implement **Alt+Click+Drag** to create a **column/rectangular selection**:
    - Translate drag rectangle into a set of line/column positions.
    - Generate a cursor per line at the corresponding column, clamping to line length.
    - Handle variable-width characters and tabs consistently with the existing visual column model.
  - Make shortcut bindings configurable or at least centralized so they can be adjusted for different platforms (e.g., Cmd on macOS).

- Edit and command application semantics
  - Modify text insertion, deletion, and navigation commands so they operate on **all active cursors**:
    - Character insertions: insert at each cursor, updating all cursor positions appropriately.
    - Backspace/delete: remove characters/selection for each cursor independently, skipping cursors where the operation is not valid (e.g., at start of document).
    - Arrow navigation/home/end/page keys: move each cursor independently, maintaining a per-cursor preferred column for vertical motion.
  - Ensure that operations that conceptually require a single range (e.g., select-all, paste when clipboard has a single string) still produce intuitive behavior:
    - For simple text paste with multiple cursors: insert the same clipboard content at each cursor.
    - For future extensibility, keep the API open to support multi-segment clipboard content (different paste per cursor) even if not implemented now.
  - Decide how structural/AST-aware edits (e.g., bold toggle, list item manipulation) should behave under multiple cursors; initially, either:
    - Restrict multi-cursor usage to raw-mode-like operations, or
    - Support multiple cursors only within the same block type, failing gracefully or collapsing to single cursor if a conflict is detected.

- Integration with existing editor modes and list editing
  - Ensure multi-cursor state can coexist with both **Raw** and **Rendered** modes; for rendered mode, treat each cursor as an independent edit point mapped to its corresponding AST node.
  - Reuse the corrected key/index mapping logic from the list-editing tasks so that multiple cursors in lists do not confuse item identity.
  - Confirm that activating multi-cursor mode does not re-introduce the "edits applied to wrong list item" class of bugs; keep item identity and cursor identity clearly separated.

- Undo/redo and history model
  - Update the undo/redo stack so a **single multi-cursor editing action** (e.g., typing a word across N cursors) is recorded as one compound operation.
  - Ensure that undo correctly restores all cursor positions and selections to their previous multi-cursor configuration.
  - Verify that the history model still plays nicely with existing list-editing history behavior added in prior tasks.

- Rendering and performance
  - Optimize rendering to handle dozens of cursors without noticeable lag (batch drawing, avoid per-cursor layout recomputation where possible).
  - Ensure focus outlines, IME composition, and text input handlers all work correctly when multiple cursors exist; for IME, consider restricting composition to the primary cursor initially.

- UX and discoverability
  - Add a brief help tooltip or documentation snippet in the editor help/shortcuts list describing:
    - Ctrl+D: add cursor to next occurrence.
    - Ctrl+Click: add cursor at click.
    - Alt+Click+Drag: column selection.
  - Ensure that exiting multi-cursor mode is intuitive (e.g., pressing Escape clears back to a single primary cursor).
tasks.testStrategy:
- Keyboard/shortcut behavior
  - With a simple text buffer, place the cursor on a word, press Ctrl+D repeatedly, and verify that each occurrence gets its own cursor/selection and that no duplicates are created.
  - Confirm that Ctrl+D stops adding cursors once all non-overlapping occurrences are selected.
  - Click to set a single cursor, then Ctrl+Click in multiple locations and verify that new cursors are added without removing existing ones; a plain click should reset to a single cursor.
  - Hold Alt and Click+Drag vertically to create a column selection; verify that:
    - A cursor is created on each line intersected by the drag.
    - Cursors align visually in a column and clamp appropriately at line ends.

- Editing semantics
  - With multiple cursors active, type text and confirm all cursors insert the same text and maintain correct positions.
  - Use Backspace/Delete with multiple cursors, including at document boundaries, and verify no panics or cross-line corruption occur.
  - Paste a single text snippet with multiple cursors and verify it appears at each cursor.
  - Perform navigation commands (arrow keys, Home/End) and ensure each cursor moves appropriately and maintains its own preferred column.

- Interaction with lists and rendered mode
  - In rendered mode with list items and nested lists, place multiple cursors across different list items and edit text; verify that:
    - Each change is applied only to the corresponding item.
    - No list item content is swapped or mis-attributed.
  - Repeat similar tests in raw mode and confirm there is no regression from pre-multi-cursor behavior for single-cursor editing.

- Undo/redo
  - Perform a multi-cursor insertion (e.g., add a suffix to a word across multiple lines) and check that a single Undo reverts all insertions and restores all cursor positions.
  - Perform multiple steps of mixed single- and multi-cursor edits and verify redo/undo sequences behave predictably and do not desynchronize cursor state.

- Stress and edge cases
  - Create 20–50 cursors (e.g., via Ctrl+D or column selection) and perform typing and deletion; verify the editor remains responsive and rendering stays correct.
  - Test multi-cursor operations at file start/end, on empty lines, and in lines with wide characters or tabs.
  - Verify that pressing Escape (or the chosen key) exits multi-cursor mode and returns to a single cursor without leaving stray selections.

- Cross-mode regression checks
  - Run existing list-editing regression scenarios (from related tasks) with both single and multiple cursors to ensure no new index/key regressions.
  - Verify that scroll behavior, selection highlighting, and focus indicators are unaffected when there is only a single cursor (i.e., default behavior is unchanged).

tasks.title: Implement crash-safe session tab and editor state restore
tasks.description: Persist the full editor session state (open tabs, active tab, scroll and cursor positions, and unsaved content recovery) and restore it on normal restart or after a crash.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:
- Define session state model
  - Design a serializable session state structure capturing at minimum: list of open documents/tabs (file path or document ID, temporary/unsaved flag), active tab ID, per-tab editor mode (Raw/Rendered), scroll offsets for each relevant pane, and per-tab cursor/selection positions.
  - Include a version field and migration strategy so future schema changes do not break restore of older sessions.
  - Represent unsaved changes explicitly (e.g., in-memory diff/snapshot or full buffer text) so recovery flows can distinguish between clean files and dirty buffers.

- Implement session state capture
  - Add hooks in the tab manager and editor components to produce a full snapshot of the current session (open tabs, active tab, per-tab editor state, scroll positions, cursors/selections, and dirty flags).
  - Reuse existing scroll synchronization and raw/rendered abstractions so captured scroll offsets and modes align with how Task 71 and 68 maintain consistent positions and behavior.
  - For each open document, capture:
    - File identity (path/ID) and last saved revision/mtime (if available).
    - Current editor mode (Raw/Rendered) and any mode-specific state needed for consistent restore.
    - Scroll position(s) for relevant views, including both raw and rendered scroll offsets when both are visible.
    - Primary cursor and selection range(s); design the API so it can be extended to multiple cursors once Task 72 lands, but do not depend on multi-cursor being present at runtime.
  - Decide on efficient snapshot frequency: on focus changes between tabs, on debounced content changes, and on clean app shutdown, minimizing I/O while keeping recovery reasonably up to date.

- Persist session state to disk
  - Choose a storage location (e.g., user config/data directory) and file format (e.g., JSON) for session snapshots and separate recovery payloads for unsaved content.
  - Implement atomic writes (e.g., write to temp file then rename) to avoid corrupting the session file on crash or power loss.
  - Encrypt or restrict permissions for the session data if it can contain sensitive content; at minimum, ensure OS-level user-only permissions on the session/recovery files.
  - Maintain two logical artifacts:
    - Last clean session snapshot (taken on orderly shutdown where all state is consistent).
    - Crash-recovery snapshot(s) that are periodically updated while the app is running.

- Implement restore flow on startup
  - On app launch, detect presence of a crash-recovery snapshot vs a clean session snapshot and distinguish between:
    - Fresh start (no prior state or user explicitly chose not to restore).
    - Normal restore (from last clean snapshot after an orderly shutdown).
    - Crash restore (from last crash-recovery snapshot after an unclean exit).
  - Recreate tabs based on the chosen snapshot:
    - Reopen documents (skipping ones that no longer exist on disk, and marking them as missing/unsaved if recovery content exists).
    - Restore active tab and previously focused pane (raw vs rendered) for that tab.
    - Restore scroll offsets for raw and rendered views using the same mapping assumptions that Task 71 stabilizes, to avoid jarring jumps.
    - Restore cursor and selection positions.
  - If the on-disk file has changed since the snapshot (mtime mismatch or checksum difference), mark the tab as conflicted and prefer safety: show both the recovered content and the on-disk version or prompt the user to choose.

- Unsaved changes and recovery UX
  - Define a clear UX for crash recovery:
    - On detecting a crash-recovery snapshot, show a non-intrusive dialog/banner summarizing recovered tabs and indicating which ones had unsaved changes.
    - Provide options such as: "Restore session", "Discard recovery data", and optionally per-tab actions.
  - For each tab with unsaved content:
    - Restore the recovered buffer into the editor, clearly indicating that it comes from recovery (e.g., banner in the tab: "Recovered unsaved changes").
    - Avoid silently overwriting a newer on-disk version; instead, allow the user to compare and decide (minimal viable: warn and require explicit confirmation to overwrite).
  - Ensure that dismissing recovery or choosing to discard it deletes or archives the crash-recovery files to avoid repeated prompts.

- Integration with existing editor behaviors
  - Ensure session persistence and restore do not break raw mode behavior, formatting edits, list handling, or undo/redo that Tasks 68 and related tasks verify.
  - When restoring scroll positions, call into the same synchronization logic used for bidirectional scroll sync (Task 71) to keep raw/rendered panes correctly aligned.
  - For now, only restore the primary cursor; once multi-cursor (Task 72) is implemented, extend the model to save and restore all cursors without changing the storage schema (e.g., store an array but currently with a single element).

- Error handling and resilience
  - Handle invalid or partially corrupted session files gracefully by:
    - Attempting a best-effort restore of valid portions.
    - Falling back to a clean start if the snapshot is unusable, with a user-visible notice.
  - Add logging/telemetry hooks (if available) for session save, restore, and failure events to aid debugging.
  - Ensure that session save operations are non-blocking relative to user input, using background tasks or async I/O with appropriate throttling.
tasks.testStrategy:
- Unit tests for serialization and persistence
  - Create tests that serialize representative session states (multiple tabs, mixed raw/rendered modes, various scroll offsets and selections) and then deserialize them, asserting structural and value equality.
  - Verify versioned schema handling: load an older-version snapshot and confirm the migration logic yields a valid current session state.
  - Test atomic write logic by simulating partial writes or interrupted saves and ensuring the loader either uses the last known good snapshot or reports a safe failure mode.

- Integration tests for basic session restore
  - Start the app, open multiple documents, switch some to rendered mode, scroll each pane to different positions, change the active tab, and then trigger a simulated clean shutdown; restart and verify:
    - The same set of tabs are reopened, in the same order.
    - The previously active tab is active on restart.
    - Raw and rendered scroll positions for each tab match pre-shutdown positions (within reasonable tolerance of any mapping logic from Task 71).
    - Cursor and selection for the primary tab are restored correctly.
  - Repeat with a mix of saved and unsaved tabs and confirm that dirty flags are preserved after restore.

- Crash-recovery behavior tests
  - Simulate a crash by killing the process after edits but before an orderly shutdown, ensuring that the periodic snapshot mechanism had time to run.
  - On next launch, verify that a crash-recovery prompt appears and that choosing "Restore session" recreates the previous tabs and editor states.
  - Confirm that unsaved edits made since the last manual save are present in recovered buffers.
  - Verify that choosing to discard recovery deletes or archives the crash-recovery data and that subsequent launches no longer show a recovery prompt.

- Unsaved changes and conflict handling tests
  - Create a file, open it in the app, make unsaved changes, allow a session snapshot, then modify the file externally on disk.
  - Restart and confirm the app detects the mismatch and:
    - Does not silently overwrite the newer on-disk file.
    - Presents a clear indication of conflict or requires explicit user confirmation before saving over the on-disk version.
  - Verify that tabs for missing files (deleted on disk after snapshot) are either marked as recovered unsaved documents or clearly identified as missing, with the ability to save them under a new name.

- Regression tests around raw/rendered behavior and scroll sync
  - With raw and rendered panes open for the same document, scroll to various positions and confirm that after restore, scroll sync remains stable and behaves as validated in Task 71.
  - Run the existing test suites from Task 68 to ensure raw mode editing, formatted content, and undo/redo sequences behave identically before and after enabling session persistence.

- Manual exploratory testing
  - Exercise edge cases such as very large documents, high-frequency edits, quickly opening/closing tabs, and toggling raw/rendered modes while session snapshots are being saved.
  - Test on different platforms or windowing environments (if applicable) to ensure session restore works consistently and does not introduce hangs or UI freezes during save/restore.

tasks.title: Implement Git integration with visual status indicators using git2
tasks.description: Add optional Git-aware UI features using the git2 crate, including gutter line change markers, current branch display, and file status badges in the file tree, all toggled off for non-Git directories.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:

1. Core Git integration service (git2-based)
- Add a new Git integration module (e.g., `git_integration` or `vcs::git`) that encapsulates all direct interactions with the `git2` crate.
- Use `git2::Repository::discover` or `Repository::open` to detect whether the current workspace root (or file’s directory) is inside a Git repo; return `None`/`Disabled` state when no repository is found.
- Define a small, UI-agnostic API, for example:
  - `enum GitStatus { Unmodified, Modified, Added, Deleted, Renamed, StagedModified, StagedAdded, StagedDeleted, Untracked, Ignored }`
  - `struct FileGitInfo { path: PathBuf, status: GitStatus, head_blob: Option<Oid> }`
  - `struct LineChange { line: u32, kind: LineChangeKind }` with `enum LineChangeKind { Added, Modified, Deleted }`.
  - `fn current_branch(&self) -> Option<String>` to return the short name of the current branch, or `None` for detached HEAD.
  - `fn file_status(&self, rel_path: &Path) -> Option<GitStatus>` wrapping `repo.status_file` or `repo.statuses`.
  - `fn line_changes(&self, rel_path: &Path, current_content: &str) -> Result<Vec<LineChange>, GitError>` to compute per-line diffs against `HEAD`.
- Implement `current_branch` using `repo.head()`, handling detached HEAD by either returning `None` or a special label like "DETACHED".
- Implement `file_status` via `git2::Repository::statuses`/`status_file`, mapping `git2::Status` flags to the internal `GitStatus` enum.
- Implement `line_changes` by:
  - Resolving the `HEAD` commit tree, finding the blob for the file path.
  - Converting the blob contents and current buffer contents to text.
  - Using `git2::Diff` (e.g., `repo.diff_blob_to_buffer`) to produce a diff and then mapping hunks/lines to 1-based line numbers in the current buffer.
  - Distinguishing **added** (lines present only in working tree), **modified** (changed lines), and **deleted** (lines in HEAD but missing in working tree; mark on the nearest following line or previous line for gutter rendering).
- Add caching/invalidation around repository and status queries to avoid excessive calls:
  - Cache last-known `Repository` instance per workspace root.
  - Cache per-file `FileGitInfo` and `LineChange` results with a short-lived TTL or invalidation on save, buffer change, or explicit refresh.

2. Configuration and toggle behavior
- Introduce a user setting (e.g., `settings.git.enabled: bool` and possibly `settings.git.indicators.*`) that globally enables/disables Git integration.
- Auto-disable Git integration when `Repository::discover` fails for the workspace root, regardless of the global flag.
- Provide a runtime toggle command (e.g., `Toggle Git Integration`) that flips the Git-enabled flag and triggers UI refresh.
- Ensure that all UI components treat Git data as optional: if the integration is disabled or not available for a given path, they must fall back gracefully with no errors.

3. Editor gutter line indicators
- Extend the editor’s rendering model to accept an optional per-line decoration input, e.g., `HashMap<u32, LineChangeKind>` or a parallel vector sized to the buffer line count.
- Define concrete visual styles for the gutter:
  - **Added**: small bar or dot in gutter with **green** color.
  - **Modified**: marker in **blue**.
  - **Deleted**: marker in **red**, placed either on the line after the deletion or on the last visible related line; ensure a consistent rule.
- On buffer load and on save:
  - If Git integration is active and the file is tracked, call `line_changes` and update the editor’s gutter decoration model.
- On text change:
  - Choose a strategy balancing accuracy and performance:
    - Eager: debounce buffer change events (e.g., 300–500 ms) and recompute `line_changes`.
    - Conservative: recompute only on save and show a "may be stale" indicator if unsaved changes exist.
  - Ensure multi-cursor and scrolling logic from other tasks are unaffected; keep git decorations read-only relative to editing state.
- Integrate with existing theme system so the three colors can be adjusted per theme (light/dark) while maintaining the required green/blue/red semantics.

4. Status bar or title bar branch display
- Add a status bar or window title entry that binds to `current_branch()`.
- When Git is enabled and a repo is available:
  - Show the short branch name (e.g., `main`, `feature/login`).
  - For detached HEAD, show a clear label such as `HEAD@<short_sha>` or `DETACHED`.
- When no Git repo is detected or Git integration is disabled:
  - Hide the branch widget or show a neutral `No Git`/blank state.
- Ensure this widget updates when:
  - The user changes branches outside the app (polling or filesystem event-based refresh, e.g., watching `.git/HEAD`).
  - The workspace root changes.

5. File tree Git status indicators
- Extend the file tree data model to hold an optional `GitStatus` per node.
- For each file/directory under the workspace root:
  - Use `file_status` to compute the Git status, using paths relative to the repo root.
  - Aggregate directory states if desired (e.g., mark a folder as `Modified` when any descendant is modified or untracked).
- Define visual indicators (icons or badges) for at least:
  - **Modified** (working tree), **Staged** (index), **Untracked**, **Ignored**.
- Apply colors consistent with standard Git conventions but readable in both light and dark themes.
- Ensure the file tree efficiently refreshes status on:
  - Initial load.
  - File save/rename/delete within the app.
  - Periodic or event-based rescan to pick up external changes.
- Provide a quick way (context menu or command palette) to force-refresh Git statuses.

6. Performance, error handling, and non-Git directories
- Make all Git operations non-blocking relative to the main UI thread; use background tasks or async jobs where appropriate.
- Debounce high-frequency triggers (typing, file change events) before hitting `git2` APIs.
- Gracefully handle errors from `git2`:
  - If the repository becomes unavailable or `.git` is corrupted, disable Git indicators for that session and surface a non-intrusive warning.
  - On permissions or IO errors, log details to a developer log but avoid crashing the UI.
- Treat non-Git directories as a first-class scenario:
  - Repository detection should be fast and cached.
  - When no repo is present, no Git-related queries or UI elements are shown beyond an optional static "Git disabled" indicator.

7. Extensibility
- Keep the Git service behind a trait (e.g., `trait VcsBackend`) so other VCS systems could be plugged in later without rewriting the UI.
- Clearly document the assumptions for line mapping (1-based lines, how deletions are represented) to avoid ambiguity when integrating with future editor features like folding or soft wrapping.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit tests for Git service (with a temporary test repo)
- Create temporary repositories with `git2` in tests:
  - Initialize a repo, add and commit a file `file.txt` with known content.
  - Modify, add, delete lines and write the modified content to the working tree without committing.
- Test `current_branch()`:
  - Verify it returns `main` (or default branch) after initial commit.
  - Create and checkout a new branch, assert the returned name updates correctly.
  - Detach HEAD (checkout a commit by ID) and assert the function returns `None` or the chosen detached representation.
- Test `file_status()` mapping:
  - Unmodified file should return `Unmodified`.
  - After editing but not adding to index, status should be `Modified`.
  - After `git add`, status should be `StagedModified`/`StagedAdded`.
  - New untracked file must return `Untracked`.
  - Deleted but not committed file should map to `Deleted`.
- Test `line_changes()` behavior:
  - From a known original file, change specific lines to create clear **added**, **modified**, and **deleted** cases.
  - Assert that returned `LineChange` entries correctly identify the line numbers and `LineChangeKind`.
  - Verify that complete deletion of a line creates a `Deleted` marker associated with the expected neighboring line.

2. UI unit/integration tests for gutter indicators
- Open a tracked file in the editor with Git integration enabled.
- Simulate edits that:
  - Add a new line.
  - Modify an existing line.
  - Delete a line.
- After triggering the diff recomputation (save or debounced change event), assert that the editor’s decoration model contains entries with the correct line numbers and kinds.
- Verify the rendered gutter (via snapshot or DOM/UI tree inspection) uses the expected color tokens for **green** (added), **blue** (modified), and **red** (deleted).
- Disable Git integration via settings/command and confirm all gutter Git markers disappear while regular editor functionality remains intact.

3. Status bar/title bar branch display tests
- With a test repo loaded, assert that the status bar shows the active branch name.
- Change branches in the repo (via shell or API), trigger a refresh, and assert the displayed branch name updates.
- In a non-Git directory, assert that the branch indicator is hidden or displays the neutral state.

4. File tree Git status indicator tests
- Populate a test repo with multiple files and directories:
  - One unmodified tracked file.
  - One modified but unstaged file.
  - One staged file.
  - One untracked file.
- Refresh file tree Git statuses and assert:
  - Each node is annotated with the expected `GitStatus`.
  - The correct icon/badge and color are applied for each status when inspecting the rendered tree or UI representation.
- Delete or rename files and confirm the file tree updates its Git status labels appropriately on the next refresh.
- Disable Git integration and verify that no Git-specific icons are shown in the file tree, even for repos.

5. Non-Git directory and error handling tests
- Open a workspace that is not a Git repository:
  - Assert that no Git calls are made beyond an initial failed discovery.
  - Confirm that the editor, status bar, and file tree do not display Git-specific decorations.
- Introduce an error by corrupting `.git` or revoking read permissions, then trigger a status refresh:
  - Verify that the integration fails gracefully (logs error, disables indicators) without crashing the app.

6. Performance and regression checks
- Measure the time to open large files with Git integration enabled vs disabled; ensure no significant regression beyond acceptable thresholds.
- Type rapidly in a tracked file and confirm that Git diff computations respect debouncing (no one-per-keystroke recomputes).
- Run the full automated test suite, including existing editor state and scroll-related tests (Tasks 71–73), to ensure that adding Git decorations does not break existing behavior.

tasks.title: Implement Zen Mode distraction-free writing experience
tasks.description: Add a Zen Mode that hides all non-essential UI, centers the text column with generous margins, and enables typewriter-style scrolling, toggleable via menu and keyboard shortcut.
tasks.details:
Implementation steps:

1. Define Zen Mode concept and state
- Introduce a global/editor-scoped **Zen Mode** state flag in the main UI/editor state (e.g., `ui::ZenMode { enabled: bool }` or a boolean within the workspace/editor layout model).
- Decide scope: Zen Mode should apply per-window/workspace (not per-tab) so the entire editing environment becomes distraction-free.
- Ensure Zen Mode state is serializable so it can be persisted and restored by the session restore system once Task 73 is complete.

2. Layout and chrome-hiding behavior
- Identify all "chrome" regions that must be hidden when Zen Mode is enabled:
  - Tab bar
  - File tree / project explorer
  - Toolbar(s) / command bar
  - Status bar
  - Any auxiliary panels (e.g., git sidebar from Task 74, once implemented) that count as distractions.
- Refactor layout container so these regions can be programmatically hidden without breaking layout (e.g., use a top-level layout model that can toggle visibility flags instead of hard-coding them).
- Implement a layout mode switch:
  - Normal mode: existing layout.
  - Zen Mode: only the **primary editor pane** is visible, occupying the window, with internal constraints for centered text.
- Ensure transitions between modes do not recreate/destroy editors; just toggle visibility of surrounding chrome and adjust padding/margins.

3. Centered text column and comfortable margins
- In the editor rendering/layout layer, add support for a **max text column width** (e.g., 70–90 characters equivalent) and **horizontal centering**.
- Implement logic so that when Zen Mode is enabled:
  - The editor view uses a fixed maximum content width and centers it within the window.
  - Left/right margins adapt to window size; on narrow windows, allow the content to stretch but never overflow.
- Add vertical padding at top/bottom to avoid the text touching window edges and to feel visually balanced.
- Ensure this is compatible with both Raw and Rendered modes (Task 71); both should appear centered and visually consistent.
- Provide a configuration option (even if not yet wired to UI) for max column width and margins, so future settings UI can tweak them.

4. Typewriter-style scrolling behavior
- In the editor scroll logic, introduce a **typewriter mode**:
  - The active line (primary caret line) should be kept **vertically centered** whenever possible.
  - On input (typing, backspace, Enter) or cursor move commands, adjust scroll position so that the active line is at vertical center of the viewport.
- Implement reasonable constraints:
  - Near the start of the document, avoid overscrolling above the first line.
  - Near the end, allow the last lines to scroll so that the active line is as centered as content allows, but do not scroll past document end.
- Integrate with existing scroll sync logic between Raw/Rendered views (Task 71):
  - When Zen Mode + typewriter mode is on, define which view drives scroll (likely the Raw editor) and ensure the mapping still holds.
  - Prevent scroll feedback loops by avoiding mutual updates when the change was initiated by typewriter centering.
- Make typewriter behavior active only in Zen Mode by default; leave a clear hook to allow separate typewriter toggle later if desired.

5. Input handling and keyboard shortcuts
- Add a **menu item** under View (or similar) named "Zen Mode" with a checkmark/indicator when active.
- Implement a **toggle command** for Zen Mode in the command palette / action system.
- Bind keyboard shortcuts for toggling:
  - Primary: `F11` (if not already used for a global full-screen toggle), or reuse an existing full-screen mechanism by extending its behavior into Zen Mode.
  - Secondary: `Ctrl+Shift+Z` (or platform-appropriate variant on macOS, e.g., `Cmd+Shift+Z` if it does not conflict with redo).
- Ensure shortcuts are properly registered in the central keybinding registry and that conflicts are resolved or documented.

6. Visual polish and theme considerations
- In Zen Mode, consider slightly dimming background chrome that still must exist (e.g., OS title bar) using available theming options, without breaking accessibility.
- Respect light/dark mode and existing contrast fixes (Task 70): margins and background colors must maintain adequate contrast with text.
- Hide non-essential inline UI adornments (e.g., gutter icons, Git change markers from Task 74) while in Zen Mode, or provide a simple flag on the gutter to render minimal-only elements.

7. State persistence and integration with session restore
- Expose Zen Mode state through the same configuration/session layer used by Task 73 for session restore:
  - On toggle, update the persistent UI state model.
  - On app startup, read last session state and restore Zen Mode if it was active, reapplying layout and typewriter mode.
- Ensure that toggling Zen Mode does not conflict with other editor modes (multi-cursor from Task 72, raw/rendered from Task 71); it should be orthogonal and not modify buffer content.

8. Error handling and fallbacks
- If layout fails to compute centered margins (e.g., very small window), gracefully fall back to a regular full-width editor view while still hiding chrome.
- Ensure that toggling Zen Mode is fast and does not cause noticeable flicker; batch layout updates where possible.
- Log or surface non-fatal layout issues only in debug/dev builds to avoid distracting users.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual and automated test plan:

1. Basic behavior
- Open a normal project with tabs, file tree, toolbar, and status bar visible.
- Toggle Zen Mode via menu and verify that tabs, tree view, toolbar, status bar, and other chrome are hidden, leaving only the editor content.
- Toggle Zen Mode off and verify all previously visible UI elements return to their prior state and positions.

2. Centered text column and margins
- In Zen Mode, resize the window from narrow to very wide and verify the text column remains centered with comfortable, increasing side margins and a fixed max text width.
- Check both Raw and Rendered markdown views to confirm both are centered and visually consistent.
- Verify that on very small windows the layout gracefully degrades without clipping text or breaking scrolling.

3. Typewriter scrolling
- Enable Zen Mode in a long document and place the caret in various positions; type and use arrow keys/PageUp/PageDown.
- Confirm the active caret line remains vertically centered whenever possible while scrolling through the document.
- At document start, verify no overscroll occurs above the first line; at document end, verify scroll stops appropriately without blank space beyond content.

4. Interaction with scroll synchronization
- With both Raw and Rendered views visible (if applicable in Zen Mode or when temporarily leaving Zen Mode), scroll in Raw while typewriter mode is active and confirm the Rendered view stays in sync without oscillations or jitter.
- Verify that scroll events triggered by typewriter centering do not cause feedback loops.

5. Keyboard shortcut and menu integration
- Toggle Zen Mode using the primary shortcut (F11 or chosen key) and verify it always matches the menu check state.
- Toggle using the secondary shortcut (Ctrl+Shift+Z or platform variant) and confirm identical behavior.
- Check that shortcuts do not interfere with existing keybindings (e.g., redo) and that conflicts are resolved per design.

6. Multi-feature compatibility
- With multi-cursor enabled (Task 72), verify that typewriter centering uses the primary caret and behaves correctly while multiple cursors are present.
- With Git integration enabled (Task 74, once available), verify that gutter indicators and file tree badges are hidden or minimized appropriately in Zen Mode and restored when Zen Mode is disabled.
- Confirm that light mode contrast and separator changes from Task 70 still look correct when Zen Mode is off, and that Zen Mode’s centered layout maintains acceptable contrast.

7. Persistence and restore
- Activate Zen Mode, close the application, and reopen it (after Task 73 is implemented) to verify Zen Mode state is restored and the layout, centered column, and typewriter behavior are all active.
- Deactivate Zen Mode, exit, and relaunch to ensure it starts in normal mode.

8. Regression checks
- Ensure that toggling Zen Mode does not cause loss of unsaved changes, cursor positions, or tab order.
- Verify performance remains acceptable: toggling Zen Mode repeatedly should not cause noticeable lag or memory growth.

tasks.title: Fix Search-in-Files result navigation highlight behavior
tasks.description: Ensure that clicking a Search in Files result scrolls the editor to the exact match location and applies a temporary visual highlight that disappears on scroll, edit, or any mouse click.
tasks.details:
Implementation plan:

1. Understand current Search in Files navigation
- Locate the code path that handles click/activation on a search result entry (likely in the search panel / search service and editor navigation logic).
- Identify how the current behavior positions the cursor and/or scrolls the editor when a result is clicked (e.g., using line/column offsets or byte ranges).
- Confirm how multiple editors (Raw vs Rendered markdown views from Task 71) are represented, and ensure the navigation always targets the correct active pane.

2. Define a temporary match highlight model
- Introduce a transient **search match highlight** concept into the editor view model, e.g., a struct like `SearchHighlight { range: TextRange, expires_on: HighlightExpiryCondition }`.
- Represent range in the same coordinate system used by the editor (line/column or character offsets) to reuse existing selection/decoration infrastructure.
- Define expiry conditions: `OnScroll`, `OnEdit`, `OnMouseInteraction`, and possibly an optional time-based expiry (e.g., fade after N milliseconds) if desired later.

3. Integrate highlight rendering with the editor
- Extend the editor rendering layer to draw a distinct background highlight for the match range that is visually different from standard selection (e.g., a softer yellow with no caret).
- Add a decoration layer or dedicated highlight style class so themes can override the color while keeping semantics (e.g., `search-match-focus`).
- Ensure highlight does not interfere with text selection: keep it a background-only decoration, independent of selection state.

4. Wire search result clicks to scrolling and highlighting
- On click of a search result item:
  - Resolve the corresponding file/tab and open it if necessary.
  - Move the primary cursor to the start of the match range.
  - Scroll the editor such that the match is visible and preferably centered or near the middle of the viewport (respecting any existing scroll-sync behavior from Task 71 for Raw/Rendered views).
  - Install a single `SearchHighlight` instance covering exactly the matched text range.
- If a different result is clicked, replace any existing search highlight rather than stacking multiples.
- Avoid persisting this highlight in session restore (Task 73) by keeping it strictly transient/UI-level state.

5. Implement highlight expiry triggers
- Scroll expiry:
  - Subscribe to the editor’s scroll events; on any vertical or horizontal scroll (excluding the programmatic scroll that occurs immediately after clicking a result), clear the current `SearchHighlight`.
  - Use a simple guard flag to ignore the initial programmatic scroll that centers the match.
- Edit expiry:
  - Hook into the editor’s text-change events; as soon as any edit occurs in the document containing the highlight (insert, delete, replace), remove the highlight.
- Mouse-click expiry:
  - Listen for mouse down / click events within the editor viewport.
  - On any mouse down inside the editor (or optionally anywhere in the window), remove the highlight.
- Ensure that clearing the highlight is idempotent and safe even if no highlight is currently active.

6. Coordinate with multi-cursor and selection behaviors (Task 72)
- Ensure the highlight is independent of multi-cursor selections:
  - Do not treat the highlight as an additional cursor/selection; render it as a separate decoration layer so that multi-cursor editing remains unaffected.
- If Ctrl+D or other multi-cursor commands are immediately used after navigating to a result, verify that they operate from the primary cursor position as normal while the highlight is purely visual.

7. Raw vs Rendered mode considerations (Task 71)
- When the search result originates from the raw text (which it likely does), ensure that highlighting occurs in the Raw editor view using raw text coordinates.
- If the Rendered view is active when the result is clicked, decide and document the behavior (recommended: switch to Raw mode when jumping to a result to avoid mismatch between rendered layout and raw text positions).
- Make sure scroll sync logic doesn’t immediately clear the highlight: use the same guard as for programmatic scroll, so the sync-induced scroll does not trigger highlight removal.

8. State lifecycle and cleanup
- Store the `SearchHighlight` in a clearly scoped, ephemeral field within the editor state (e.g., per-tab UI state), not in persisted models.
- On tab switch, file close, or editor re-open, ensure any active highlight is dropped rather than restored.
- Consider adding a small helper API (e.g., `editor.set_search_highlight(range)` and `editor.clear_search_highlight(reason)`) to centralize highlight logic and removal reasons for debugging.

9. Accessibility and visual design
- Choose a highlight color with sufficient contrast against typical backgrounds, distinct from selection and Git diff markers (Task 74) and multi-cursor decorations (Task 72).
- Ensure that highlight rendering is performant for large files: rendering a single small range should be negligible; avoid re-layout of the whole buffer.
- Optionally expose a setting for users to disable or customize this temporary highlight, but default it to enabled.
tasks.testStrategy:
Manual and automated tests:

1. Basic navigation and highlight
- Run a Search in Files for a term that appears multiple times across several files.
- Click a result in a file that is already open:
  - Verify the target file/tab becomes active.
  - Verify the editor scrolls so that the match is clearly visible (preferably near the center of the viewport).
  - Verify the exact matched text range is visually highlighted with the new temporary highlight style, distinct from text selection.
- Click a result in a file that is not open:
  - Verify the file opens in a new tab and the same scroll + highlight behavior occurs.

2. Highlight expiry on scroll
- After navigating to a result and seeing the highlight, use the mouse wheel or trackpad to scroll up or down.
- Confirm that the highlight disappears as soon as the user-initiated scroll occurs.
- Repeat using keyboard-based scrolling (Page Up/Down, arrow keys if they trigger scroll) and verify the highlight is also removed.
- Confirm that the initial automatic scroll that brings the match into view does not clear the highlight prematurely.

3. Highlight expiry on edit
- Navigate to a result and observe the highlight.
- Type a character, delete a character, or paste text anywhere in the document (inside or outside the highlighted region).
- Verify that the highlight is immediately removed on the first edit.

4. Highlight expiry on mouse click
- Navigate to a result and observe the highlight.
- Click with the mouse anywhere in the editor area (inside or outside the highlighted region).
- Verify that the highlight disappears upon the click and does not reappear.

5. Repeated navigation behavior
- Click one search result, then—without changing search terms—click another result in the same file and then a result in a different file.
- Confirm that:
  - Each time, the editor scrolls to the new match location.
  - Only one highlight is ever visible at a time (the previous one is cleared when the new one is applied).
  - No ghost highlights remain when switching between tabs.

6. Interaction with multi-cursor (Task 72)
- With multi-cursor functionality enabled, navigate to a search result.
- Use Ctrl+D (or equivalent) to select the current word and subsequent occurrences.
- Verify that:
  - Multi-cursor selections appear and behave as expected.
  - The temporary search highlight is independent: it may be cleared by the first edit, but it does not interfere with selection rendering or cursor placement.

7. Interaction with Raw/Rendered views and scroll sync (Task 71)
- With both Raw and Rendered modes available, perform a Search in Files while in Raw mode and click a result.
- Verify that:
  - The highlight appears in the Raw editor at the correct text.
  - Scroll synchronization between Raw and Rendered is not broken and does not immediately remove the highlight beyond the defined conditions.
- If the implementation chooses to auto-switch to Raw mode when jumping from a result while in Rendered mode, verify that this switch happens predictably and that the highlight appears at the correct raw location.

8. Interaction with Git decorations and other markers (Task 74)
- In a Git-tracked file with gutter changes and status indicators active, perform a Search in Files.
- Confirm the temporary search highlight remains visually distinguishable from Git change markers and selection highlights.
- Verify that enabling/disabling Git integration does not affect the behavior of the search highlight.

9. Regression checks
- Ensure that existing editor search (within-file find/replace) still works as before, and its own highlights/selections are not affected by the new temporary highlight.
- Confirm that session restore (Task 73) does not restore temporary search highlights after an application restart.
- Run automated UI/integration tests (if available) that simulate search result clicks, scrolling, editing, and mouse interactions, asserting that the highlight state appears and disappears according to the rules above.

tasks.title: Implement configurable auto-save with toolbar toggle and temp file backups
tasks.description: Add an auto-save feature that saves documents after a configurable idle delay when enabled, controlled by a toolbar toggle and settings option, using a temp-file-based strategy to prevent data loss without overwriting the main file prematurely.
tasks.details:
Implementation plan:

1. Define configuration and defaults
- Introduce new settings for auto-save under the editor/project settings model, for example:
  - `auto_save_enabled_default: bool` (default `false`).
  - `auto_save_delay_ms: u32` (e.g., default 15000 ms, with reasonable min/max bounds like 1000–300000).
- Ensure these settings integrate with the existing configuration system and persist across sessions alongside other editor/UI options (align with how settings from tasks 71–73 are stored, but do not depend on them directly).
- Decide precedence rules:
  - A per-document or per-session **runtime state** flag `auto_save_enabled` in the editor instance, initially set from `auto_save_enabled_default` on open.
  - Changes via the toolbar toggle only affect the current editor/session instance, not the global default.
